coredata.dc:title: G-C3N4 nanosheets adhered with Ag3BiO3 and carbon dots with appreciably promoted photoactivity towards elimination of several contaminants
coredata.prism:copyright: © 2021 The Society of Powder Technology Japan. Published by Elsevier B.V. All rights reserved.
coredata.prism:publisher: The Society of Powder Technology Japan. Published by Elsevier B.V.
coredata.dc:creator: Asadzadeh-Khaneghah, Soheila
Habibi-Yangjeh, Aziz
Seifzadeh, Davod
Chand, Hushan
Krishnan, Venkata
coredata.dc:description: Herein, graphitic carbon nitride nanosheets (signified as CN-NS) were anchored with Ag3BiO3 and carbon dots (abbreviated as CDs) by hydrothermal procedure with appreciably promoted photoactivity towards elimination of several contaminants. The CN-NS/CDs/Ag3BiO3 (30%) system, as optimal photocatalyst, demonstrated extremely high elimination efficiency for several pollutants such as methylene blue (MB), malachite green (MG), Rhodamine B (RhB), and methyl orange (MO) under visible light, which is 55.5, 36.5, 39.2, and 32.1 folds premier than CN material, respectively. Besides, PL, EIS, and transient photocurrent response data corroborated that the CN-NS/CDs/Ag3BiO3 (30%) material has the highest charge carriers segregation efficiency compared with the pure and binary systems. Energy band structure analysis demonstrated that the improved performance of CN-NS/CDs/Ag3BiO3 photocatalyst is allocated to Z-scheme heterojunction. Scavenging tests demonstrated that h+, •OH, and •O2 − are the crucial species in the RhB degradation. Enhancement of elimination ability can be allocated to more generation of the charge carries and improvement of the segregation rate of them promoted through the Z-scheme mechanism.
coredata.dcterms:subject: g-C3N4 nanosheet/CDs/Ag3BiO3
Visible-light photocatalysis
Z-Scheme heterojunction

item-info.ce:copyright: The Society of Powder Technology Japan

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a) XRD patterns of as-constructed systems. (b) EDX spectrum of the CN-NS/CDs/Ag3BiO3 (30%) system.
(a) TEM and (b) HRTEM images related to the CN-NS/CDs/Ag3BiO3 (30%) system.
(a) XPS survey spectrum and the spectra of: (b) C 1s, (c) N 1s, (d) Ag 1s, (e) Bi 4f, and (f) O 1s in the CN-NS/CDs/Ag3BiO3 (30%) system.
FT-IR spectra of the as-constructed systems.
(a) RhB degradation and (b) the elimination rate constants of RhB on the materials.
UV–vis spectra for elimination of RhB under visible light over the (a) CN-NS, (b) CN-NS/CDs, and (c) CN-NS/CDs/Ag3BiO3 (30%) systems.
(a) UV–Vis DRS, (b) TGA, (c) BET, and (d) pore size distribution curves for as-prepared photocatalysts.
(a) PL spectra, (b) Transient photocurrent curves, and (c) EIS plots for the as-prepared samples.

(a) Mott–Schottky plot for the Ag3BiO3, (b) Impact of various quenchers on the removal rate constants of RhB on the CN-NS/CDs/Ag3BiO3 (30%) system. (c) Schematic representation for segregation of charges in the CN-NS/CDs/Ag3BiO3 photocatalysts.
The removal rate constants for MB, RhB, MG, and MO on the as-prepared systems.
Schematic image for fabrication of CN-NS/CDs/Ag3BiO3 photocatalysts.

head.ce:title: G-C3N4 nanosheets adhered with Ag3BiO3 and carbon dots with appreciably promoted photoactivity towards elimination of several contaminants

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Chemistry, Faculty of Science, University of Mohaghegh Ardabili, Ardabil, Iran
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Department of Chemistry, Faculty of Science, University of Mohaghegh Ardabili, Ardabil, Iran

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Nanoscience and Nanotechnology Research Group, Water Engineering and Mineral Waters Research Center, University of Mohaghegh Ardabili, Ardabil, Iran

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Water Engineering and Mineral Waters Research Center

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Nanoscience and Nanotechnology Research Group, Water Engineering and Mineral Waters Research Center, University of Mohaghegh Ardabili, Ardabil, Iran

ce:affiliation.ce:label: c
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Basic Sciences and Advanced Materials Research Center, Indian Institute of Technology Mandi Kamand, Mandi 175075, Himachal Pradesh, India

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: School of Basic Sciences and Advanced Materials Research Center
Indian Institute of Technology Mandi Kamand

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: School of Basic Sciences and Advanced Materials Research Center, Indian Institute of Technology Mandi Kamand, Mandi 175075, Himachal Pradesh, India

ce:correspondence.ce:text: Corresponding author at: Department of Chemistry, Faculty of Science, University of Mohaghegh Ardabili, Ardabil, Iran.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Herein, graphitic carbon nitride nanosheets (signified as CN-NS) were anchored with Ag3BiO3 and carbon dots (abbreviated as CDs) by hydrothermal procedure with appreciably promoted photoactivity towards elimination of several contaminants. The CN-NS/CDs/Ag3BiO3 (30%) system, as optimal photocatalyst, demonstrated extremely high elimination efficiency for several pollutants such as methylene blue (MB), malachite green (MG), Rhodamine B (RhB), and methyl orange (MO) under visible light, which is 55.5, 36.5, 39.2, and 32.1 folds premier than CN material, respectively. Besides, PL, EIS, and transient photocurrent response data corroborated that the CN-NS/CDs/Ag3BiO3 (30%) material has the highest charge carriers segregation efficiency compared with the pure and binary systems. Energy band structure analysis demonstrated that the improved performance of CN-NS/CDs/Ag3BiO3 photocatalyst is allocated to Z-scheme heterojunction. Scavenging tests demonstrated that h+, •OH, and •O2 − are the crucial species in the RhB degradation. Enhancement of elimination ability can be allocated to more generation of the charge carries and improvement of the segregation rate of them promoted through the Z-scheme mechanism.

ce:section.ce:para: With quick growth of population and industrialization, the water pollutants created through organic contaminants are becoming a serious global problem that threatens human safety and health. Different synthetic organic compounds (e.g. pesticides, herbicides, dyes, personal care products, pharmaceuticals, etc.) are discharged into effluents and finally arrive natural water sources. It is well known that most of these materials are persistent organic contaminants, which threaten the living organisms, including human being [1–3]. Hence, several biological and physical purification procedures have been utilized for the elimination of these contaminants from water, for example ultrafiltration, adsorption, and coagulation [4,5]. However, possible secondary pollution and also insufficient capacity to eliminate traces of these contaminants restrict their practical applications. Hence, environmentally friendly and highly efficient treatment processes are needed for the elimination of organic contaminants from water. Advanced oxidation processes (AOPs) have attracted a lot of interest from researchers, due to their high efficiency in removing and even mineralizing organic contaminants [6]. Furthermore, AOPs are more environmentally friendly than biological and physical treatment processes as they could convert organic contaminants to nontoxic compounds and they do not release masses of deleterious residues [7]. The outstanding versatility and activity of AOPs are arisen from the generation of highly reactive species like •O2 − and •OH, which can impressively attack the target pollutants causing their degradation [8,9]. Hence, many attempts have been done to exploitation of durable and efficient heterogeneous photocatalysts, as effective AOPs [10–13].

G-C3N4 (abbreviated as CN) has lately utilized as suitable photocatalyst in AOPs owing to its low toxicity and cost, facile synthesis, good stability, superior oxidation ability, and unique electronic structure [14,15]. As a metal-free conjugated polymer, the moderate optical gap (~2.7 eV) of CN endows it with a good light absorption characteristic [16]. Unfortunately, CN still suffers from moderate photoactivity because of the sluggish photocarrier transfer, insufficient light absorption, and small surface area [17,18]. The limitations of CN can be dominated by heterojunction construction, elemental doping, morphology control, and textural improvement [19–23]. In this work, among mentioned procedures, texture modification utilized to increase surface of CN. For this aim, nanosheets of CN (denoted as CN-NS) were constructed with a simple heating way. As a new group of carbon nanomaterial, carbon dots (CDs) were affirmed to be an electron acceptor for diminishing the electron/hole pairs segregation [24–26]. Thus, combining CN-NS and CDs led to heterojunctions with excellent photoactivity. In addition, coupling of CN-NS with semiconductors containing narrow band gap is an efficacious strategy to manipulate other drawbacks of CN. Because, the adhering of appropriate semiconductors onto the surface of CN-NS helps to ameliorate the separation of electron/hole pairs and enhance the visible-light absorption, and thus leading to generation of further reactive species.

Ag-based semiconductor photocatalysts have attracted special interests because of the oxygen permeability, broad light absorption, and good conductivity [27]. As a novel semiconductor, Ag3BiO3 has appropriate band energies with optical gap of 1.86 eV [28]. To our best knowledge, there is not any report about design of CN-NS/CDs/Ag3BiO3 systems and investigation of their photocatalytic ability. In this research paper, CDs and Ag3BiO3 were adhered on the surface of CN-NS through hydrothermal procedure, and the photoactivity of the systems for organic contaminants removal under visible-light illumination was assayed. Various pollutants were chosen as model contaminants to verify the photoactivity of the constructed systems. The durability of the nanocomposites was also assayed. Moreover, the major active species contributing to photocatalytic activity were determined. Finally, the conceivable Z-scheme mechanism was specified via different technical analysis and characterization.

The XRD measurements were done on Philips Xpert using Cu Kα radiation. The UV–Vis DRS were obtained by Scinco 4100 spectrophotometer. The HRTEM and TEM images were gained through a Technai G 20 (FEI) S-twin microscope. The PL and FT-IR spectra were recorded on a Perkin Elmer (LS 55) and Perkin Elmer Spectrum RX I instruments, respectively. The surface elemental composition of the synthesized materials were explored by ThermoFisher Scientific XPS (NEXSA). The UV–Vis spectra for the removal reaction were assayed via a Cecile 9000 instrument. The N2 adsorption and desorption isotherms were obtained with BELSORP-mini II adsorption instrument. The TGA data were obtained by Linseis STA PT 1000 apparatus. The electrochemical data were gained by a standard three-electrode setup, as described in the literature [29].

The CDs, CN-NS, and CN were constructed pursuant to the formerly represented literatures [29]. The CN-NS/CDs/Ag3BiO3 (30%) system was constructed by hydrothermal procedure as follows. At first, 0.350 g CN-NS/CDs and 0.125 g Bi(NO3)3·5H2O were dispersed ultrasonically in water and stirred for 0.5 h. Then, a solution consisting 2 mL HNO3 and 0.132 g AgNO3 were appended to the above-constructed suspension under stirring for 0.5 h. After that, 10 mL NaOH (5 M) was slowly appended to the suspension upon stirring for 0.5 h. Lastly, the obtained product was transferred into 200 mL Teflon-lined stainless steel autoclave. The system was sealed and protected at 179 °C for 12 h. The obtained materials were washed with ethanol and water and then dried at 60 °C for one day. The schematic image for construction of the CN-NS/CDs/Ag3BiO3 (30%) system is seen in Scheme 1 .

The photoability of as-fabricated systems was appraised at 25 °C under visible light of a 50 W LED lamp for removal of MG, RhB, MO, and MB through recording the absorptions at 616, 553, 477, and 664 nm, respectively. The other information was entirely explained in our former research literature [29].

To realize the crystal structural characteristics, XRD patterns of the nanomaterials are represented in Fig. 1 a. For the CN sample, the in-planar peak is seen at 13.2° and the typical interlayer stacking peak is measured at 27.4° (JCPDS card no. 87-1526) [29]. About CN-NS, the position of the CN sharp peak, put at 27.4°, is enhanced till 27.7°, which expresses denser packing due to the successful exfoliation. In addition, the lesser intense peak in the CN-NS compared to the CN could be due to the delamination of the planes [30]. But, according to the XRD pattern of the CN-NS/CDs composite, the diffraction peaks related to CDs are absent, because of its very low amount [31]. The XRD pattern of ternary CN-NS/CDs/Ag3BiO3 (30%) system contains the diffraction peaks of CN-NS and Ag3BiO3 [32], which affirm the purity of the as-constructed material. Moreover, the XRD patterns express that the constructed systems are crystalline. Also, the CN-NS/CDs/Ag3BiO3 (30%) nanocomposite was more assayed from the point of view of elemental composition. The EDX spectrum observed in Fig. 1b obviously demonstrates the existence of N, C, O, Bi and Ag elements in the nanocomposite.

To more unveil the structure of the as-constructed CN-NS/CDs/Ag3BiO3 (30%) system, TEM image was taken, as presented in Fig. 2 a. The TEM image illustrates well adhering of CDs and Ag3BiO3 nanomaterials on the CN-NS. The CN-NS/CDs/Ag3BiO3 (30%) hybrid is more corroborated through the HRTEM image as observed in Fig. 2b. According to Fig. 2b, the CN-NS/CDs/Ag3BiO3 (30%) sample presents the well-defined crystalline planes, which confirm high crystallinity of as-fabricated ternary system [32–34]. Because as we know, the striped pattern in the HRTEM image evidenced crystalline structure.

To determine the oxidation state of the elements in the CN-NS/CDs/Ag3BiO3 (30%) system, XPS analysis was considered. In Fig. 3 a, the peaks for N, C, O, Bi, and Ag can be seen. The XPS spectra of C 1s, N 1s, Ag 3d, Bi4f, and O1s are observed in Fig. 3(b–f). From Fig. 3b, it can be observed that the XPS spectrum of C 1s is deconvoluted to three peaks, at about 288.6, 285.8, and 284.7 eV. The peaks at 284.7and 288.6 eV originate from the bonded carbon at sp2 CC and sp2 NCN bonds that are the main species assigned to C element in the CN-NS, respectively [31,35]. The peak at 285.8 eV is attributed to the COH bond, which arises from CDs [31]. The N 1s spectrum (Fig. 3c) is deconvoluted to two peaks put at 400.8and 398.4 eV, which are allocated to the N of N(C)3 and CNC groups, respectively [36]. In Fig. 3d, the spectrum of Ag 3d can be deconvoluted to two peaks at374.4and 368.4 eV, which are allocated to Ag 3d3/2 and Ag 3d5/2, respectively [37]. Fig. 3e demonstrates the Bi 4f XPS spectrum; the two peaks are put at 165.3and 159.4 eV, which are allocated to the Bi4f5/2 and Bi4f7/2, respectively [38]. Fig. 3f exhibits the spectrum of O1s. The distinct peaks at 534.2 and 532.4 eV are assigned to the O element of CO and COC bonds existing in CDs, respectively [39]. Moreover, the peak located at 529.7 eV corresponds to O 1s of O2− [38]. Finally, the XPS data affirm co-existence of CN-NS, CDs, and Ag3BiO3 in the ternary photocatalyst.

Fig. 4 demonstrates the FT-IR spectra taken from the materials. In the spectra of all samples, the absorption peaks centered around 804, 1200–1633, and 3000–3400 cm−1 are allocated to bending vibration of the heptazine unit, stretching vibration of the CN heterocyclic, and the adsorbed H2O molecules, respectively [35]. For the CN-NS/CDs system, the peaks devoted to the CO stretching vibration and also the CH2 bending vibration are seen in 1600–1770 cm−1 and 1431 cm−1, respectively [40,41]. For the CN-NS/CDs/Ag3BiO3 (30%) system, the existence of Ag3BiO3 is affirmed through the observation of absorption peak regarding BiO at 525 cm−1 [42].

After the successful fabrication and detailed characterization of constructed samples, they were utilized for the elimination of RhB, and the outcomes are demonstrated in Fig. 5a. To assay the stability of RhB under visible light, a blank test was done without any photocatalyst (photolysis test). The efficiency of the photolysis is almost 8.5%, indicating that RhB has considerable stability under the light source. After 60 min, the yield of decomposition of RhB was obtained to be 100% in the existence of CN-NS/CDs/Ag3BiO3 (30%) system, which demonstrates the supreme photoactivity; while, after 60 min, the degradation percentage over the CN-NS/CDs, Ag3BiO3, CN-NS, and CN photocatalysts are only 49.2%, 48.1%, 44.0%, and 17.3%, respectively. More interestingly, the photoactivity of CN-NS/CDs/Ag3BiO3 system reduces with increasing amount of Ag3BiO3 above 30 wt%, representing that 30 wt% is the optimal amount of Ag3BiO3 in the ternary system. Because, by loading more Ag3BiO3, the heterojunctions between CN-NS/CDs and Ag3BiO3 are destructed, due to the aggregation of Ag3BiO3 nanoparticles. Hence, segregation of the electron/hole pairs could not occur impressively, leading to diminish of the photoactivity. Furthermore, determination optimum composition for the components in photocatalysts is usually required, because weight percentages of the components could affect on the surface area, pore sizes, and light absorption capability, which are important parameters affecting on the photocatalytic activity. For comparison purpose, the CDs/Ag3BiO3 (30%) and CN-NS/Ag3BiO3 (30%) nanocomposites were also applied to photodegradation of RhB under the same conditions. The results obviously displayed that the degradation efficiencies are very lower than the ternary CN-NS/CDs/Ag3BiO3 nanocomposites. These results suggest that the photocatalytic performance is significantly enhanced by the heterojunction formations among CN-NS, CDs, and Ag3BiO3 components. The CN-NS/CDs/Ag3BiO3 (30%) system demonstrates the highest degradation rate constant of 665 × 10−4 min−1, which is 39.2, 15.8, 8.74, 6.30, 11.4, and 14.7 folds of CN (17.0 × 10−4 min−1), CN-NS (42.0 × 10−4 min−1), Ag3BiO3 (76.1 × 10−4 min−1), CN-NS/CDs (106 × 10−4 min−1), CN-NS/Ag3BiO3 (30%) (58.3 × 10−4 min−1), and CDs/Ag3BiO3 (30%) (45.1 × 10−4 min−1) photocatalysts, respectively, as observed in Fig. 5b. The excellent photoactivity of CN-NS/CDs/Ag3BiO3 (30%) system can be allocated to quick charge carrier segregation and higher light absorption ability, which is in accord with EIS, photocurrent response, PL, and UV–Vis DRS results given in the following sections.

Changes in UV–vis absorbance spectra of RhB as a function of excitation time in the existence of as-constructed systems are illustrated in Fig. 6 (a–c). Clearly, the absorption peak of RhB alleviates quickly on CN-NS/CDs/Ag3BiO3 (30%) system with prolonged light illumination (Fig. 6c). While, only 49.2% and 44.0% of RhB were removed over the CN-NS/CDs and CN-NS systems, respectively. Thus, it is noted that the synergetic effect of the components in the photocatalyst plays a main task for boosting the photoability.

In this part, UV–vis DRS was utilized to assay optical features of the photocatalyst. After investigating the optical abilities of as-fabricated samples, we saw that absorption edges of synthesized photocatalysts are in the visible area (Fig. 7 a). Compared with the pristine CN, the CN-NS sample exhibits absorption blue-shift, which proves exfoliation of the fabricated CN-NS [43]. Also, in contrast to the CN-NS, the Ag3BiO3 demonstrates obviously increased absorption in the visible area with an absorption edge larger than 700 nm. Moreover, as can be seen, the light absorption capacity of the CN-NS/CDs/Ag3BiO3 (30%) system, as optimum ternary photocatalyst, substantially increased in the visible region, and shows the strongest light absorption capacity. This property could assist more impressive visible-light usage for the elimination of pollutants. To estimate the relative mass ratio of the organic part in the fabricated samples, TGA was done from 25 °C to 700 °C under air atmosphere (Fig. 7b). As it can be observed, graphitic carbon nitride starts to decompose at about 490 °C and illustrates a sharp weight loss from 550 °C to 650 °C [44] and the thermostability of this organic semiconductor is diminished after converting to CN-NS and coupling with CDs and Ag3BiO3 nanoparticles. For the CN-NS, binary, and ternary systems, the adhered nanoparticles and increased surface area through exfoliation could act as active sites for adsorption of O2 from the surrounding, causing to oxidize CN part of the samples at less temperatures than the pristine CN [44]. The accurate amount of graphitic carbon nitride in the CN-NS/CDs/Ag3BiO3 (30%) system was gained through determining the weight loss of the photocatalyst in the TGA diagram, which is 71.3 wt%. Fig. 7c demonstrated the N2 adsorption–desorption isotherms of the materials. The curves displays hysteresis characteristics, which demonstrate that mesoporous structures exist in the photocatalysts. The surface areas of CN-NS/CDs/Ag3BiO3 (30%), CN-NS/CDs, CN-NS, and CN are 41.2, 51.2, 88.1, and 14.6 m2 g−1, respectively. The enhanced surface area of CN-NS demonstrates sever exfoliation of this organic semiconductor. While, after loading CDs and Ag3BiO3 nanoparticles, the surface area of CN-NS was alleviated, which is allocated to blocking some of the active sites of the CN-NS by CDs and Ag3BiO3 particles. Even though the CN-NS/CDs/Ag3BiO3 (30%) nanocomposite do not have the highest surface area, it exhibited the excellent photoactivity for RhB elimination. These data exhibit that boosted photoability of the ternary nanocomposite allocated to more absorption of visible light. Furthermore, the pore size distribution curves for the as-prepared photocatalysts were gained with the BJH method, representing mesoporous characteristics (Fig. 7d).

The movement and recombination of charges in the as-fabricated photocatalysts were ascertained by PL, transient photocurrent, and EIS analyses. PL emission arisen from the recombination of charges when the samples are illuminated and indeed their intensity express the recombination rate of charges. The PL spectra showed in Fig. 8 a, were obtained by illumination of the materials with an excitation wavelength of 325 nm. Among the photocatalysts, CN, CN-NS, CDs, and Ag3BiO3 have the highest emission peaks, demonstrating the highest recombination rate of charge carriers. While, compared with pure and binary systems, the PL intensity of CN-NS/CDs/Ag3BiO3 (30%) system demonstrated clear diminishes, indicating that the successful construction of heterojunction can impressively promote the charge carriers segregation and movement in the ternary photocatalyst. In order to give other proof to the impressive charge carriers segregation in the CN-NS/CDs/Ag3BiO3 (30%) sample, photocurrent response were carried out. As depicted in Fig. 8b, the CN-NS/CDs/Ag3BiO3 (30%) system has superior light current response, indicating that the optimum ternary nanocomposite has the maximum segregation efficiency for the charge carriers and the minimum recombination rate. Furthermore, after four on/off periods of light irradiation, the ternary nanocomposite demonstrated reproducible and prompt photocurrent responses in each period, demonstrating the excellent durability of the photocatalyst. Finally, in Fig. 8c, the EIS data depicts that the CN-NS/CDs/Ag3BiO3 (30%) nanocomposite has the smallest semicircle radius among the Nyquist plots, indicating that the constructed heterojunction has the lowest resistance for the migration of charge carriers, which leads to the reduced recombination of the electron-hole pairs in the ternary sample, as confirmed by PL spectra and photocurrent densities.

To ascertain band-edge potentials of prepared samples, Mott-Schottky measurements were done (Fig. 9 a). The slope of the plot was positive for Ag3BiO3, demonstrating its n-type nature [45]. Based on the Mott-Schottky data, the flat-band potentials was obtained to be +0.09 for Ag3BiO3 vs. Ag/AgCl. In n-type semiconductors, the conduction band (CB) was more negative by ca. 0.1 eV than the flat band potential. So, the Ef of Ag3BiO3 relative to the Ag/AgCl and NHE potential is +0.09 V and +0.29 V, respectively. Hence, the CB position of Ag3BiO3 was obtained to be +0.19 eV. While, the CB of CN-NS (−1.25 eV) has been calculated elsewhere [29]. Finally, the valence band (VB) was obtained based on EVB = ECB + Eg; the VB amounts of CN-NS and Ag3BiO3 obtained to be 1.55 and 2.05 eV, respectively. To more accurate understand the promoted photocatalytic ability of CN-NS/CDs/Ag3BiO3 nanocomposites, the main active species for RhB elimination was found through the active species scavenging tests. As observed in Fig. 9b, ammonium oxalate (AO: h+ quencher), 2-propanol (2-PrOH: •OH quencher), and benzoquinone (BQ: •O2 − quencher) clearly quenched the elimination efficiency, which demonstrated that the active species were the crucial species in the RhB elimination.

As shown in Fig. 9c, the promoted photocatalytic elimination mechanism could be put forward through a Z-scheme electron/hole pairs migration pathway. As depicted, when the CN-NS/CDs/Ag3BiO3 system is illuminated by visible light, the electrons move from the VB to CB in CN-NS and Ag3BiO3 semiconductors, respectively. Then, the excited electrons on the CB of Ag3BiO3 flow into CDs nanoparticles. Afterwards, electrons move from the CDs nanoparticles to the VB of CN-NS, causing the accumulation of electrons in the CB of CN-NS and holes in the VB of Ag3BiO3. In such a way, the electrons and holes gather on the CB of CN-NS and VB of Ag3BiO3, respectively. Since the E(O2/•O2 −) is more positive than the CB of CN-NS, hence, the electrons on the CB of CN-NS could react with O2 molecules to produce superoxide anion radicals. The holes on the VB of Ag3BiO3 could react with OH− to generate hydroxyl radicals, because the E(OH−/•OH) is more negative than the VB of Ag3BiO3. Under the action of abundant strong oxidizing species, RhB is decomposed into elimination products, which is in good agreement with the quenching tests.

As we know, the durability of a nanocomposite is crucial for its practical applications. So, for assay the stability, the cyclic removal of RhB on the CN-NS/CDs/Ag3BiO3 (30%) system was done under the same reaction conditions. As observed in Fig. 10 , after four consecutive recycles, the photocatalyst illustrates excellent reusability with slight deactivation. This diminish can be allocated to slight loss of the loaded particles on the CN-NS during washing and separation process. Hence, it is concluded that the CN-NS/CDs/Ag3BiO3 (30%) system has structural durability and acceptable photoability for application in photocatalytic processes.

To demonstrate the promoted activity of this novel ternary photocatalyst for elimination of various organic pollutants, three kinds of contaminants, containing MB, MO, and MG were selected for photocatalytic degradation by the as-prepared systems, as depicted in Fig. 11 . Clearly, it can be observed that the CN-NS/CDs/Ag3BiO3 (30%) system presents more pronounced activity toward removal of MB, MG, and MO by visible light excitation. Meanwhile, the pure and binary systems also show much lower activity than the CN-NS/CDs/Ag3BiO3 (30%), which indicates collaboration between CN-NS, CDs, and Ag3BiO3 counterparts in considerably enhancing photocatalytic activity. It is noteworthy that the boosted activity of the ternary photocatalyst is 55.5, 39.2, 36.5, and 32.1-folds as high as the CN in removals of MB, RhB, MG, and MO, respectively. The outstanding performance of novel CN-NS/CDs/Ag3BiO3 photocatalyst displays its capability to remove various contaminants from the environment.

In briefly, ternary Z-scheme CN-NS/CDs/Ag3BiO3 photocatalysts were constructed through a simple hydrothermal procedure. Various characterizations denoted the complete fabrication of the heterojunction photocatalyst through anchoring Ag3BiO3 and CDs onto CN-NS. The CN-NS/CDs/Ag3BiO3 nanocomposite demonstrated the highest photoactivity for the elimination of several pollutants by visible-light excitation. In addition, the activity of CN-NS/CDs/Ag3BiO3 (30%) nanocomposite towards degradation of RhB was about 39.2, 15.8, and 6.30 times of CN, CN-NS, and CN-NS/CDs, respectively. It is noteworthy that CDs served as the electron movement mediator between CN-NS and Ag3BiO3 through Z-scheme mechanism, resulting in promoted segregation of the generated charges in CN-NS and Ag3BiO3 components. Simultaneously, the better redox ability could be reserved, consequently promoting the photoactivity. This research paper provided an innovative viewpoint for preparing Z-scheme systems using CN-NS, Ag3BiO3, and CDs that could be utilized for the photocatalytic effluent treatment.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: We are honestly thankful regarding the fiscal support from University of Mohaghegh Ardabili, Iran.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: A critical review on textile wastewater treatments: possible approaches

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: C.R. Holkar, A.J. Jadhav, D.V. Pinjari, N.M. Mahamuni, A.B. Pandit, A critical review on textile wastewater treatments: Possible approaches, Journal of Environmental Management 182 (2016) 351–366.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: TiO2-based photocatalytic disinfection of microbes in aqueous media: a review

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: P.V. Laxma Reddy, B. Kavitha, P.A. Kumar Reddy, K.-H. Kim, TiO2-based photocatalytic disinfection of microbes in aqueous media: A Review, Environmental Research 154 (2017) 296–303.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Carbonaceous-TiO2 nanomaterials for photocatalytic degradation of pollutants: a review
